name: Germany
summary: Strong long-game: dual citizenship is retained both ways (Germany permits multiple nationality since 27 June 2024 and Bangladesh grants a Dual Nationality Certificate), five-year naturalisation, an unusually fast EU Blue Card → settlement-permit PR (21 months with B1 German, 27 with A1), tuition-free public master's and immediate statutory healthcare. The binding weaknesses are the entry stage and climate — a roughly 27-month student-visa appointment backlog at the German Embassy Dhaka, the second-highest tax wedge in the OECD with no expat relief, a B1-German burden for status, and a rising-Islamophobia / strong-AfD (20.8%, second place) political environment.

links.title: Make it in Germany (official federal portal)
Student visa & finances — German Embassy Dhaka (official)

job-market.summary: Largest EU economy with a chronic IT skills shortage (software qualifies for the lower Blue Card threshold) and several tech hubs, but much of the market is German-language-gated and 2024–26 hiring softened.

pros.text: IT/software is a recognised shortage (bottleneck) occupation, so software roles qualify for the lower EU Blue Card salary threshold (€45,934.20 in 2026) — easily cleared by two engineers.
Median software-developer pay is ~€65,250 (StepStone 2025, ~€71,250 at firms >5,000 staff), with a ~15–25% premium for AI/ML skills.
Multiple hubs — Berlin, Munich, Hamburg, Frankfurt, Stuttgart, plus Tübingen/Cyber Valley and research institutes (Max Planck, Fraunhofer, DFKI) — can absorb both engineers.

cons.text: Outside English-friendly Berlin/startups and MNCs, much of the market still gates roles on German (B1–B2), limiting real hireability for a non-German-speaking Bangladeshi graduate.
Tech hiring softened over 2024–26 and absolute pay trails the US/Switzerland/Australia even after the AI premium.

links.title: Make it in Germany — EU Blue Card (official federal portal)
heise online — Salary Report: what IT professionals earn (StepStone 2025)

visa-access.summary: A full embassy in Dhaka, but the binding constraint is a ~27-month student-visa appointment backlog (still current June 2026); add the APS academic check, a ~€11,904 blocked-account proof and genuine-student scrutiny.

pros.text: Visas are processed at a full German Embassy in Dhaka — no travel to New Delhi — and there is no Bangladesh-specific ban or visa pause; diplomatic ties are stable.
Genuine, well-documented students with admission and funds are routinely approved; the decision is rules-based rather than a high-refusal regime.

cons.text: Student-visa appointment waits at the German Embassy Dhaka run to roughly 27 months as of June 2026 — a severe entry bottleneck that can delay relocation by years.
Bangladeshi applicants must clear an APS (Akademische Prüfstelle) certificate plus genuine-student scrutiny, and prove a blocked account of about €11,904 for one year before lodging.

links.title: German Embassy Dhaka — Student visa (official)
German Embassy Dhaka — Student-visa checklist (official PDF)

citizenship.summary: Five years' residence, B1 German plus a civics test, dual citizenship retained both ways, and a top-tier passport; the only recent negative is the 2025 repeal of the three-year fast-track.

pros.text: Dual citizenship works both ways: Germany's modernised nationality law permits multiple citizenship since 27 June 2024, and Bangladesh grants a Dual Nationality Certificate (Citizenship Act §2B(2)), so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality.
Standard naturalisation is reachable in five years' lawful residence, and the German passport ranks 4th on the Henley 2026 index (185 visa-free destinations).

cons.text: The 2025 coalition repealed the three-year fast-track for the exceptionally well-integrated, so five years is now the floor for this profile.
Naturalisation requires B1 German plus an Einbürgerungstest and proof of self-sufficiency — a real language hurdle for two English-working engineers.

links.title: BMI — Modernisation of nationality law (official)
Bangladesh MOFA — Dual Nationality Certificate (official)

post-study-work.summary: Graduates of German universities get an 18-month residence permit to seek qualified work, with unrestricted work while searching and a deterministic offer-based conversion to a Blue Card; both spouses qualify in their own right.

pros.text: Graduates of a German university receive an 18-month residence permit to look for qualified employment, with unrestricted work allowed during the search.

Conversion is deterministic and offer-based: a job at the IT/shortage Blue Card threshold converts the permit straight into a Blue Card — no lottery — and each graduating spouse qualifies independently.

cons.text: The 18-month runway is shorter than the 2–3 years offered by English-native destinations, and finding qualified work fast can be gated by German for many roles.

links.title: BAMF — University graduates / job-seeking residence permit (official)
Make it in Germany — EU Blue Card (official federal portal)

spouse-family.summary: Spouses of Blue Card / skilled workers get unrestricted labour-market access with no German-language precondition, full family reunification and free public schooling, plus a clear path to independent status.

pros.text: The spouse of an EU Blue Card or skilled-worker holder may work without restriction, and — unusually — without first proving German-language skills.
Family reunification is privileged for Blue Card holders; children attend free public schools, and the spouse can move onto their own work/Blue Card and PR track.

cons.text: Bringing a spouse on the student phase requires extra proof of funds, and a spouse joining from Bangladesh faces the same severe Dhaka appointment backlog.

links.title: Make it in Germany — Family reunification (official federal portal)
Make it in Germany — EU Blue Card (official federal portal)

msc-study.summary: Tuition-free public master's (only a ~€250–350 semester fee; Baden-Württemberg charges non-EU €1,500/sem) with a deep English-taught CS/AI catalogue; admission for a BD BSc-CSE graduate is attainable but gated by the APS check.

pros.text: Public universities charge no tuition (only a ~€250–350 semester contribution), so a CS/AI master's is essentially free — a decisive cost advantage over Australia/UK/US.

Wide English-taught CS/AI offering (roughly 40–58 master's programmes at institutions such as TU Munich, RWTH Aachen and TU Darmstadt), and any German degree qualifies for the 18-month post-study permit.

cons.text: Bangladeshi applicants must obtain an APS certificate and clear degree recognition/uni-assist, and top programmes are competitive.
Students may work only ~140 full / 280 half days a year (~20 hrs/week), capping study-phase earnings.

links.title: DAAD — International Programmes in Germany (official)
German Embassy Dhaka — Student-visa checklist incl. APS (official PDF)

pr-pathway.summary: An unusually fast statutory PR: an EU Blue Card holder reaches a settlement permit in 21 months (B1 German) or 27 months (A1), with the IT salary bar easily met — the language level is the real gate, not the timeline.

pros.text: EU Blue Card holders obtain a permanent settlement permit after just 21 months with B1 German (27 months with A1) — among the fastest skilled-PR timelines in Europe.
The route is statutory and clear (no points lottery); two engineers comfortably clear the salary and pension-contribution criteria.

cons.text: PR still requires at least A1 German (B1 for the 21-month track) plus continuous pension contributions, so the language requirement is the binding hurdle.
The 2025 government's broader migration tightening adds some policy uncertainty around the edges, even if the skilled route remains stable.

links.title: Make it in Germany — Settlement permit (official federal portal)
Service Berlin — Settlement permit for EU Blue Card holders (official)

income-cost.summary: Two tech salaries leave a comfortable but not large surplus after Germany's heavy tax-and-contribution load, and Munich/Berlin housing is pricey and tight; tuition-free study eases the overall cost burden.

pros.text: A dual tech household (~€120k–140k gross combined) supports a comfortable lifestyle and steady remittance to Bangladesh under the Germany–Bangladesh tax treaty.
Tuition-free public study materially lowers the total cost of the migration plan versus English-native destinations.

cons.text: Housing in the tech hubs is expensive and supply-tight: ~€1,300/month for a one-bed in Berlin and ~€1,500+ in Munich, with stiff competition for flats.
High taxes and social contributions trim net pay, so savings are solid rather than spectacular.

links.title: Studying in Germany — Cost of living (2026)
OECD — Taxing Wages 2025: Germany (country note, PDF)

healthcare.summary: High-quality statutory healthcare with immediate coverage for both students (~€140/month) and workers (auto-enrolled, contributions split with the employer), plus a strong welfare and parental-benefit safety net.

pros.text: Newcomers are covered immediately: students under 30 join statutory insurance at ~€140/month (2026), and employees are auto-enrolled in GKV from day one with contributions (~14.6% + ~1.7%) split with the employer.
Strong safety net — unemployment support after contributions, pensions, and generous parental cash benefits (Elterngeld).

cons.text: Specialist and GP appointment waits can be long, and subsidised childcare (Kita) places are often scarce in big cities.

links.title: How to Germany — Statutory health insurance for students (2026)
How to Germany — Statutory health insurance: eligibility & contributions

culture-language.summary: English works in tech hubs and the workplace but German is needed for daily life and for B1-level status; excellent work-life balance and funded integration courses, set against mixed social acceptance.

pros.text: Excellent work-life balance (28–30 days' leave is common) and high livability, with state-funded integration courses (language + civics) for newcomers.
English is workable in tech workplaces and English-friendly Berlin, easing the first years.

cons.text: German is needed for everyday life and bureaucracy, and B1 is required for PR/citizenship — a sustained language investment.
Social acceptance of South-Asian newcomers is mixed and the climate is hardening alongside the AfD's rise.

links.title: Make it in Germany — Learning German (official federal portal)
BAMF — Integration courses (official)

safety-law.summary: Top-tier rule of law (WJP 2025: 6th, 0.83) and broadly safe (GPI 2025: 20th), but anti-Muslim hate crime is rising sharply and anti-discrimination enforcement has documented gaps.

pros.text: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 6th of 143 (0.83); fair, court-backed immigration due process and a stable legal order.
Global Peace Index 2025: 20th of 163 — broadly safe everyday environment.

cons.text: Anti-Muslim incidents are rising fast (CLAIM: 3,080 documented in 2024, +60% year-on-year; ~930 Islamophobic crimes recorded by police Jan–Sep 2025).
Human Rights Watch flags under-enforcement of anti-Muslim racism, so anti-discrimination law is not always matched by protection on the ground.

links.title: World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index 2025 (Germany)
Global Peace Index 2025 (PDF)

politics.summary: Institutionally stable (CDU/CSU–SPD coalition under Chancellor Merz from May 2025) and still actively recruiting skilled non-EU workers, but the AfD's surge to 20.8% and second place and a hardening sentiment trend raise volatility.

pros.text: Stable governance: a CDU/CSU–SPD coalition with Friedrich Merz elected Chancellor on 6 May 2025, and the Skilled Immigration Act keeps actively courting skilled non-EU workers (Blue Card, Opportunity Card, lower IT threshold).

cons.text: The AfD took 20.8% and 151 seats (second largest party) in the February 2025 election, normalising anti-immigration and anti-Muslim politics.
The broader sentiment trend is hardening (asylum/family-migration tightening, repealed citizenship fast-track), even as the skilled route stays open.

links.title: 2025 German federal election (results overview)
deutschland.de — Federal election 2025 result & government (official)

tax.summary: The second-highest tax wedge in the OECD (47.9% for a single average earner) with no special expat regime; offset by a Germany–Bangladesh double-tax treaty and low study-phase exposure.

pros.text: A Germany–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty is in force, and study-phase earnings largely fall under the ~€12,096 tax-free allowance, keeping student tax exposure low.

cons.text: Germany's tax wedge is the second-highest in the OECD — 47.9% for a single average earner (Taxing Wages 2025) — heavily reducing net pay.
There is no 30%-style expat tax regime, and Ehegattensplitting gives little benefit to two roughly equal earners.

links.title: OECD — Taxing Wages 2025: Germany (country note, PDF)
PwC — Germany: foreign tax relief and tax treaties

muslim-diaspora.summary: Western Europe's second-largest Muslim population (~5.5m) with abundant mosques and halal food, but the Bangladeshi-specific community is modest and anti-Muslim sentiment is rising sharply.

pros.text: Around 5.5 million Muslims (second-largest in Western Europe) mean mosques, prayer facilities and halal food are abundant in every city.
A fast-growing South-Asian presence (e.g. ~41,000 Indian nationals in Berlin by 2024) supports groceries, community organisations and events.

cons.text: Anti-Muslim sentiment is rising sharply (CLAIM documented 3,080 incidents in 2024, +60%), and survey data shows widespread anti-Muslim attitudes.
The Bangladeshi-specific community is modest and Berlin-concentrated — far smaller than in the UK or Italy — and some Länder restrict headscarves for public-sector staff.

links.title: CLAIM — Anti-Muslim Racism Report 2024 (summary, PDF)
Anadolu Agency — 930 Islamophobic crimes Jan–Sep 2025 (government data)
